title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 26, 2026
headline: UN inquiry finds Israel committed genocide against Palestinian children; 20,000 killed since October 2023.
summary: June 26 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: 15 assessments, zero score changes, and eight false-alarm signals stopped — doubling the previous single-night record set June 25. The cycle's lead finding is a landmark UN document. On June 23, the UN Commission of Inquiry (report A/HRC/62/CRP.2) found that Israel 'continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children.' About 20,000 Palestinian children have been killed since October 2023; about 44,000 have been injured. The finding is Israel's own conduct and reinforces the floor score of 0 of 100. No lower score is possible. Two other developments mark escalations from the prior cycle. The Democratic Republic of the Congo's (DRC) Ebola outbreak has reached Europe: a humanitarian doctor in France tested positive on June 24 — the first EU case imported from this outbreak. The outbreak now has 1,118 confirmed cases and 291 deaths, the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak in African history. Sudan's siege of El Obeid entered its fourth week: Human Rights Watch published an action brief on June 26 documenting RSF drone strikes on power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering — 50 or more civilians killed. Two countries are newly assessed this cycle. In Yemen, the Houthi de facto authorities forced the World Food Programme (WFP) to end contracts with all 365 local staff in northern Yemen — the area covering 70 percent of humanitarian needs. Seventy-three UN workers remain detained; one has died in custody. In South Sudan, about 10 million people — two-thirds of the population — need humanitarian assistance, with state forces obstructing aid delivery. The June 26 deadline for the US Congress to receive an explanation of the Anthropic export-control order passed with no public response from Commerce Secretary Lutnick. Anthropic's score holds at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. The eight signals stopped before they could affect scores covered every major screening rule: a floor-limitation block on Israel, an attribution check on Lebanon, a double-count block on Nigeria, a three-rule compound on Anthropic, and pre-adjudication holds on OpenAI, UnitedHealth, and Apple.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: UN Inquiry Finds Genocide Against Palestinian Children — About 20,000 Killed
topSignals.whyItMatters: A UN Commission of Inquiry found Israel committed genocide by deliberately targeting Palestinian children. About 20,000 children are dead. Israel is already at the lowest possible score and cannot go lower — but the evidence record is deepening with a new legal category.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at zero of 100 — the lowest possible score. On June 23, the UN Commission of Inquiry (report A/HRC/62/CRP.2) released a finding that Israel 'continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children.' The inquiry documented approximately 20,000 Palestinian children killed and 44,000 injured since October 2023. About 30 percent of the people killed in Gaza were children. The inquiry documented attacks on neonatal and maternity centers, torture of detained children, and killings that continued after the October 2025 ceasefire. This is Israel's own conduct — scorable under the benchmark's attribution rule. The floor score of zero reflects the most severe possible designation. No lower value exists. The genocide finding is a new evidence category (a formal legal characterization by a UN treaty-body inquiry), distinct from the prior conflict-death and war-crime evidence streams. It reinforces the floor on the Awareness (AWR), Empathy (EMP), Equity (EQU), Accountability (ACC), and Integrity (INT) dimensions. The finding does not propose a score change because no lower score is possible — but it deepens the evidentiary record in a qualitatively new way.

evidence.quote: continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children
evidence.claim: UN Commission of Inquiry report A/HRC/62/CRP.2 (June 23, 2026) finds Israel commits genocide by deliberately targeting children; approximately 20,000 killed, 44,000 injured since October 2023; killings continued after the October 2025 ceasefire.
UN Commission of Inquiry full report A/HRC/62/CRP.2: genocide and atrocity crimes findings including attacks on neonatal centers, torture of detained children, post-ceasefire killings.

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: El Obeid Siege Enters Week Four — HRW Demands UN Security Council Act
topSignals.whyItMatters: Drone strikes hit displacement shelters and a funeral in a besieged city of 500,000 people. Sudan is at the lowest possible score. The UN Security Council warned of imminent mass atrocities six days ago. Evidence is not retreating — it is escalating.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at zero of 100. The Rapid Support Forces' (RSF) drone campaign against El Obeid in North Kordofan has now run for 14 to 26 consecutive days. Human Rights Watch published an action brief on June 26 demanding that the UN Security Council take bold steps: RSF drones have struck power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering. More than 50 civilians have been killed in drone strikes. The UN Security Council issued a statement on June 20 warning of 'imminent risk of mass atrocities' against El Obeid's civilian population of about 500,000. The documented RSF pattern — drone strikes on infrastructure, then direct assault on residential areas — is what killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El Fasher in three days in May 2025. A 29-country coalition at the UN Human Rights Council issued an atrocity warning on June 18. Famine has been officially declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli. Sudan's floor score reflects total state failure to protect civilians across all eight benchmark dimensions.

evidence.quote: Sudan: Urgently Address the Situation In and Around El Obeid, Take Bold Steps Towards Peace
evidence.claim: HRW June 26 action brief: RSF drone strikes day 14-26 hit power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral; 50-plus civilian deaths; UNSC action demanded.
UN Security Council statement June 20 warned of imminent risk of mass atrocities in El Obeid; 500,000-plus civilians besieged; 100,000-plus internally displaced within the city.

topSignals.title: DRC 2.3 of 100: Ebola Reaches France — 1,118 Cases, 291 Deaths, Fastest Outbreak in African History
topSignals.whyItMatters: The DRC's Ebola outbreak — already the fastest-growing on record — has now reached Europe. A humanitarian doctor in France tested positive on June 24. There is no approved vaccine. The July natural peak is now days away. The DRC government's contact tracing stands at 45 percent.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) holds at 2.3 of 100. As of June 24, the Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak has 1,118 confirmed cases and 291 deaths — up from 1,094 cases and 277 deaths two days earlier. The UN has called it the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak in African history. On June 24, a humanitarian doctor in France tested positive for Ebola — the first confirmed EU-imported case from this outbreak. A second imported case was reported in Germany. The World Health Organization (WHO) declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) on May 16. No approved vaccine exists for the Bundibugyo strain. Contact tracing is running at 45 percent — far below the 90 percent needed to stop transmission. More than 70 health workers have been infected, degrading response capacity. The DRC government's failure to achieve adequate contact tracing is own-conduct evidence scored on the Awareness (AWR) and Action (ACT) dimensions. The near-floor score of 2.3 reflects collapsed state capacity rather than deliberate state harm. The July natural Ebola peak is now within days.

evidence.quote: Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda
evidence.claim: ECDC June 24 update documents outbreak at 1,118 cases and 291 deaths — the fastest-growing Bundibugyo-strain event ever; first EU-imported case confirmed in France; Germany reported another imported case.
CNN June 24 report confirms France's first Ebola patient from the DRC outbreak — a humanitarian doctor who had worked in the DRC.

topSignals.title: Yemen 0 of 100: Houthi Authorities Force WFP to End All Northern Food Aid — 73 UN Staff Still Detained
topSignals.whyItMatters: Yemen's Houthi authorities have now forced the UN food agency to fire every local staff member in northern Yemen — eliminating food aid to the area with 70 percent of the country's humanitarian needs. One UN worker has died in custody. The governing actor is actively dismantling international aid infrastructure.
topSignals.description: Yemen holds at zero of 100. The Houthi de facto authorities in northern Yemen forced the World Food Programme (WFP) to terminate contracts with all 365 local staff in the northern area — the zone covering about 70 percent of Yemen's humanitarian needs. Aid assets have been seized. Seventy-three UN workers remain detained; one has died in custody. About 22.3 million people need humanitarian assistance across Yemen. Only 12.7 percent of the $2.16 billion UN humanitarian appeal is funded. Human Rights Watch called on the Houthis in June 2026 to release all detained UN and civil-society staff. The Houthi governing actor's actions — forced termination of food-agency staff, detention of aid workers, and seizure of humanitarian assets — are own-conduct evidence scored against the entity responsible for governing northern Yemen. These actions are reflected across the Action (ACT), Equity (EQU), Boundaries (BND), Accountability (ACC), and Integrity (INT) dimensions. The floor designation at zero reflects the total collapse of protection for civilians under Houthi governance in northern Yemen.

evidence.quote: WFP terminated all 365 staff contracts in Houthi-controlled northern Yemen
evidence.claim: UN News June 2026: WFP terminated all 365 local-staff contracts in northern Yemen (70 percent of humanitarian needs); 73 UN workers detained, one died in custody; aid assets seized; only 12.7 percent of $2.16B appeal funded.
Human Rights Watch June 7 report calls on Houthis to release all detained UN and civil-society staff; documents conditions of detention.

topSignals.title: South Sudan 0 of 100: 10 Million People — Two-Thirds of the Country — Need Humanitarian Assistance
topSignals.whyItMatters: Two-thirds of South Sudan's population needs humanitarian aid. The government obstructs relief deliveries, attacks aid workers, and pursues political purges. The score is at the lowest possible level. Two external crises — Sudan's civil war and global fuel-cost spikes — compound the situation without moving the score.
topSignals.description: South Sudan holds at zero of 100. About 10 million people — roughly two-thirds of the country's population — need humanitarian assistance. In Akobo alone, 140,000 people have been displaced; more than 300,000 are displaced across Jonglei. The WFP is serving 1.5 million fewer people than planned. Aid workers have been attacked. State forces are obstructing aid delivery through bureaucratic barriers and political purges. About 7.1 million people face crisis-level food insecurity. Two external shocks add pressure but are not scored as South Sudan's own conduct: the ongoing conflict in Sudan has disrupted the oil pipeline, and the Hormuz crisis has raised food-trucking costs by about 33 percent. South Sudan's floor score reflects the governing authority's own conduct — obstruction of aid, weaponization of access, and failure to protect civilian populations — not the external shocks.

evidence.claim: UN News June 2026: 140,000 displaced in Akobo, 300,000-plus across Jonglei, 10 million needing assistance (two-thirds of population); WFP serving 1.5M fewer than planned; state obstruction of aid documented.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 of 100: Congressional Deadline Passed With No Commerce Response — Export Controls Remain
topSignals.whyItMatters: The US Congress set a June 26 deadline for the government to explain why it suspended two Anthropic AI models. The deadline passed with no public response. The suspension stays in effect. Anthropic's score holds at 59.1 out of 100 — 0.9 points below the Established band. The government's silence is the next entry in the record.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100. Four bipartisan members of Congress set June 26 as the deadline for Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to explain the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) export-control order that forced global suspension of two Anthropic AI models — Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the first export controls ever applied to a commercially deployed AI model. As of the assessment date, no public response from Secretary Lutnick has been published. The Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models remain suspended globally, including for Anthropic employees who are foreign nationals. The benchmark's attribution rule: the US government took the action that forced the models offline. Anthropic responded by complying with the order while stating publicly that 'a narrow jailbreak does not justify global withdrawal.' That conduct is consistent with Anthropic's existing record of principled, transparent engagement under regulatory pressure. No new own-conduct evidence — positive or negative — warrants a score change this cycle. Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established band boundary (60.0). The next observable event: a public BIS response, a congressional hearing, or modification of the export-control order.

evidence.quote: June 26 Congressional deadline passed with no public Lutnick response; Fable 5/Mythos 5 remain suspended globally
evidence.claim: AI Weekly June 26 confirms the Congressional deadline elapsed with no public Commerce Department response; export-control suspension on Fable 5 and Mythos 5 remains in effect.
Washington Post June 18: bipartisan House members demanded the statutory basis for applying export controls to AI API access after Fable/Mythos controls were imposed.

topSignals.title: Somalia 4.7 of 100: Buur Hakaba Malnutrition Rate Crosses Famine Threshold — No Formal Declaration Yet
topSignals.whyItMatters: A district in Somalia has hit the malnutrition level that defines famine. No formal famine has been declared yet — that declaration is the trigger for a score change. The World Food Programme is reaching only one in ten people who need food. The window for a formal declaration is open through September.
topSignals.description: Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. The IPC/FSNAU June 2026 update documents that Buur Hakaba district's acute malnutrition rate (37.1 percent, measured by the Global Acute Malnutrition index) has exceeded the famine malnutrition threshold. The IPC frames the current situation as 'risk of Famine,' conditional on a second consecutive failed crop season. As of June 26, the IPC/FSNAU has not issued a formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration for Buur Hakaba. That formal declaration is the benchmark's scored change trigger — the methodology treats it as an adjudicated-equivalent event, not a forecast. The distinction matters: a malnutrition rate above the threshold is serious evidence of deterioration; it is not the same as a concluded IPC determination that famine conditions exist. The broader context: about six million Somalis face acute food insecurity; 1.9 million children are malnourished; WFP is reaching only one in ten people who need food; Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct food deliveries. The famine risk extends credibly through September 2026 in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions. Somalia's near-floor score of 4.7 already reflects the severity of this crisis. A formal IPC Phase 5 declaration would constitute the trigger for a scored change.

evidence.quote: risk of Famine in one location
evidence.claim: IPC/FSNAU April–June 2026 update: Buur Hakaba GAM 37.1 percent exceeds famine malnutrition threshold; IPC frames as 'risk of Famine'; second consecutive failed season would likely trigger formal declaration. About 6 million people in IPC Phase 3-plus.

International Crisis Group June 2026: WFP reaching only 1-in-10 Somalis needing food; 1.9 million children malnourished; Al-Shabaab obstructing deliveries; famine risk through September in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo.

topSignals.title: Lebanon 17.2 of 100: Amnesty's War-Crime Finding in Lebanon Is Israel's Conduct — Lebanon's Score Unchanged
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel's military forced Lebanese civilians to evacuate dozens of towns — a war crime, according to Amnesty International. That action counts against Israel's score, not Lebanon's. Lebanon is scored on what Lebanon does to its own people. Tonight's attribution check is a plain example of that rule.
topSignals.description: Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100. Amnesty International's June 2026 report found that Israel 'radically expanded' its mass evacuation orders in southern Lebanon, covering 29 towns, and committed the war crime of unlawful transfer — the forced displacement of civilians. This finding is significant. But it describes what Israel did inside Lebanon. Under the benchmark's scoring rule — we score who caused the harm — this evidence is attributed to Israel and scored against Israel's record, not Lebanon's. Lebanon's own-conduct evidence for this assessment window shows no new adverse findings in either direction. No new negative Lebanese conduct toward its own population, and no new positive conduct either. The benchmark also notes: fifth-round Israel–Lebanon ceasefire talks occurred on June 23–25; a US-brokered truce was reached on June 19. Ceasefire negotiations and their outcomes will be assessed in future cycles as evidence of Lebanese governance response capacity. Lebanon's score at 17.2 remains unchanged this cycle.

evidence.quote: Lebanon: Israel radically expands use of unlawful mass evacuation orders and commits war crime of unlawful transfer
evidence.claim: Amnesty International June 2026: Israel's mass evacuation orders covering 29 towns in southern Lebanon constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer — attributed to Israel, not Lebanon, under the benchmark's perpetrator-attribution rule.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Next US Commerce or BIS action on the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order, or a congressional hearing. The June 26 deadline passed without a public response from the Secretary of Commerce.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Six consecutive cycles in this range. The export-control order is attributed to the US government; Anthropic's transparent, principled response is consistent with its existing score. The June 26 Congressional deadline elapsed without a public Commerce response.

boundaryWatch.trigger: NLRB review of the unfair-labor-practice charge filed after Apple closed its Towson, Maryland store — its first unionized store. An NLRB complaint or ruling is the conversion trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits 0.6 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. The machinists' union charge alleges union workers received different relocation terms than non-union workers at simultaneously closing stores. The charge has not yet resulted in an NLRB complaint. Second boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: July natural Ebola peak window; M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger if obstruction of the health response is documented.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 13 of the Ebola emergency. The outbreak has now reached 1,118 cases and 291 deaths — the fastest-growing Bundibugyo event on record. First EU-imported case confirmed in France on June 24. Contact tracing at 45 percent. The July peak is days away.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Famine declaration in Buur Hakaba district. Acute malnutrition rate (37.1 percent GAM) now above the famine malnutrition threshold. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is the scored change trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia at 4.7 Critical. Buur Hakaba GAM 37.1 percent exceeds the famine malnutrition threshold; IPC frames it as 'risk of Famine' pending a second failed crop season. WFP reaching one in ten people. No formal IPC declaration as of June 26. Famine risk window extends through September.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review. Burkina Faso (6.3) was reassessed in June on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Mali's seed score of 12.5 is above the likely calibrated range.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. Not assessed this cycle. The Sahel calibration flag opened June 24 remains open. The gap with Burkina Faso (6.3, reweighted June 1 on active-state-perpetration conduct) persists. Coordinator review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and Barrows v. Humana class action (nH Predict AI claim denials). Either concluded finding confirms the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Fifth cycle post-downgrade. Not assessed this cycle. Neither the DOJ complaint nor the Barrows class action has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing. Not assessed this cycle.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through a twelfth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's RSF drone campaign against El Obeid has run for 14 to 26 consecutive days. Human Rights Watch demanded UNSC action on June 26. The UN Security Council warned of imminent risk of mass atrocities on June 20. The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes then direct assault — killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El Fasher in three days in May 2025.
emergingRisks.detail: 500,000-plus civilians remain in El Obeid with hospitals offline. Drone strikes have now hit power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering — 50-plus civilians killed. The US State Department's formal atrocity-threat warning and the 29-country UNHRC coalition warning have not changed RSF posture.

emergingRisks.risk: The DRC Ebola outbreak has spread to France — the first EU case imported from this outbreak. The July natural Bundibugyo peak is now days away. The outbreak has 1,118 cases and 291 deaths. No approved vaccine exists. Contact tracing stands at 45 percent, far below the 90 percent needed to stop transmission.
emergingRisks.detail: EU spread (France, Germany) triggers ECDC response protocols and may affect international travel-health advisories, adding pressure to WHO response capacity in eastern DRC. More than 70 health workers are infected. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu — documented obstruction by M23 would extend Rwanda's perpetrator attribution to a secondary pathway.

emergingRisks.risk: Yemen's Houthi authorities have now terminated all 365 local WFP staff contracts in northern Yemen, eliminating the primary international food-assistance mechanism for the area covering 70 percent of the country's humanitarian needs. Seventy-three UN workers remain detained; one has died in custody.
emergingRisks.detail: The next observable event is whether OCHA or IOM can establish alternative delivery mechanisms. Without food-agency staff, aid delivery to 22.3 million people who need assistance is severely constrained. The Houthi governing actor's documented conduct — forced termination of staff, seizure of assets, detention of workers — is already priced at the floor score of 0 of 100.

emergingRisks.risk: Buur Hakaba's acute malnutrition rate (37.1 percent) has crossed the famine malnutrition threshold. The IPC/FSNAU frames it as 'risk of Famine' pending a second consecutive failed crop season. A formal IPC Phase 5 declaration is the scored change trigger for Somalia — the closest time-bounded event to a score change in this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: WFP is reaching one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab is actively obstructing food deliveries. The famine risk extends credibly through September in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions. The Sahel lean season peaks in August, compounding the situation. Each new IPC Phase 5 designation is a scored change trigger.

emergingRisks.risk: OpenAI faces simultaneous proceedings from 42 state attorneys general and a Florida criminal investigation. A settlement or adverse finding in any one of these would move OpenAI's score from its current 27.5 of 100.
emergingRisks.detail: The 42-state subpoena covers data practices, handling of minors and seniors, model sycophancy, and internal safety policies — the broadest consumer-protection action ever filed against a frontier AI company. The Florida criminal investigation is tied to the FSU shooting. Both proceedings are ongoing with no fixed hearing dates.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — 37 days away. General-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps as of this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: A post-applicability non-compliance finding for general-purpose AI models constitutes a Tier-5 methodological finding and would move scores. Anthropic and DeepMind have documented compliance infrastructure. The gap between compliant and non-compliant labs will become legally significant in 37 days.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's enforced-disappearance pattern — documented by UN Special Procedures as potentially constituting crimes against humanity — remains unresolved in the Sahel calibration framework. Mali holds a seed score of 12.5 compared with Burkina Faso's reweighted 6.3 on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct.
emergingRisks.detail: The coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. New evidence (three Bamako opposition disappearances, May 8-11) adds to the documented pattern. No timeline is set for the coordinator review.

sectorAlerts.headline: Five entities at the absolute floor and four near-floor — nine total; new evidence deepens the record across all nine.
observations: Israel (0 of 100) received its strongest single-cycle evidence reinforcement tonight: the UN Commission of Inquiry found Israel committed genocide by deliberately targeting Palestinian children — approximately 20,000 killed. The floor designation is reinforced with a new legal-category determination.

Sudan (0 of 100): RSF drone siege of El Obeid entered its fourth consecutive week. Human Rights Watch demanded UNSC action June 26. Strikes hit displacement shelters and a funeral gathering — 50-plus civilians killed.

Yemen (0 of 100): The Houthi governing actor forced the WFP to terminate all 365 northern-area staff contracts, eliminating food-agency presence in the zone covering 70 percent of the country's humanitarian needs.
South Sudan (0 of 100): About 10 million people — two-thirds of the population — need humanitarian assistance. State obstruction of aid delivery continues.
DRC (2.3 of 100): Ebola reached France on June 24, the first EU-imported case. The July peak is days away. Contact tracing at 45 percent.
Somalia (4.7 of 100) is the entity closest to a score-change trigger: Buur Hakaba's malnutrition rate crossed the famine threshold; a formal IPC Phase 5 declaration remains the conversion event.

sectorAlerts.headline: Lebanon confirmed via attribution check; harm inflicted by Israel inside Lebanon counts against Israel, not Lebanon.
observations: Lebanon (17.2 of 100) confirmed this cycle via the attribution rule. The major headline — Amnesty International found Israel's mass evacuation orders in 29 southern Lebanese towns constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer — describes Israel's conduct, not Lebanon's own actions.
Lebanon is scored on what Lebanon does to its own people. The absence of new negative Lebanese own-conduct evidence is not a positive signal for Lebanon — it is neutrality.
The Lebanon case is the clearest illustration in this cycle series of the perpetrator-attribution rule: the entity that took the action is the one scored for it.

sectorAlerts.headline: Anthropic's Congressional deadline passed; the AI-lab score gap remains at 59.1 points for a sixth cycle.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100, Functional band): The June 26 Congressional deadline for the US Commerce Department to explain the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order passed without a public response from Secretary Lutnick. The suspension remains in effect. Anthropic's own conduct is consistent with its existing score.
xAI/Grok (0 of 100, Critical band): OPC Canada PIPEDA ruling and the Kim whistleblower-retaliation suit are both active. The floor score reflects confirmed conduct across multiple evidence streams.
OpenAI (27.5 of 100, Developing band): 42-state AG subpoena and Florida criminal investigation carry forward as pre-adjudication. No new proceedings this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability is 37 days away. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps. The compliance gap between labs will become legally significant on August 2.

sectorAlerts.headline: Eight signals stopped before they could distort the record — a new single-night record, double the prior mark set June 25.
observations: Floor-limitation (Israel): The UN Commission of Inquiry genocide/atrocity-crimes finding is major own-conduct evidence. Israel is at 0 of 100 — the floor. No lower score exists. The finding reinforces but cannot lower the composite.
Attribution check (Lebanon): The Amnesty war-crime finding describes what Israel did in Lebanon. Harm done to Lebanon by Israel is scored against Israel, not Lebanon. No Lebanese score change.
Double-count (Nigeria): The IPC Phase 5 Catastrophe in Borno is the same event applied in the June 19 downgrade to 18.0. Counting it again would inflate the record.

Compound three-rule (Anthropic): The rotation cache showed 60.0 (authoritative index: 59.1); the BIS order is government action on Anthropic, not Anthropic's own conduct; the June 26 deadline is a forward trigger, not within-window scored conduct.
Pre-adjudication holds (OpenAI, UnitedHealth, Apple): Investigations, probes, and charges are not concluded proceedings. Scores move when courts or regulators conclude their work.

Threshold-check (Somalia): Buur Hakaba's GAM rate (37.1 percent) crosses the malnutrition threshold but the IPC has not issued a formal famine declaration. The formal declaration is the scored change trigger, not the precursor metric.

methodologyNotes.name: Who Caused the Harm — The Attribution Rule in Practice
methodologyNotes.description: The benchmark scores the entity that caused harm — not the one that experienced it. This cycle's clearest example: Amnesty International found that Israel's mass evacuation orders in southern Lebanon constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer. That is Israel's conduct, committed inside Lebanon. The harm happened to Lebanon and its people. Under the attribution rule, we score Israel for this action. Lebanon's score reflects Lebanon's own conduct toward its own people — and nothing changed on that record this cycle. The same rule explains why the US export-control order does not lower Anthropic's score: the US government took that action, not Anthropic.

methodologyNotes.name: What the UN Genocide Finding Means for a Floor-Designated Entity
methodologyNotes.description: The UN Commission of Inquiry found that Israel commits genocide by deliberately targeting Palestinian children. Israel already scores 0 of 100 — the lowest possible value. The finding reinforces the floor but cannot lower it further. This raises a methodology question: when a floor-designated entity receives a new category of adverse finding — a formal legal-category determination (genocide), not just more evidence of the same type — does the floor designation absorb it, or does the finding establish a new dimension sub-anchor? The current methodology treats the floor as absorbing all categories. The genocide finding is logged as evidence-tier reinforcement under the Equity (EQU) and Empathy (EMP) dimensions. Whether to formalize a genocide sub-anchor as a distinct methodology category in v1.3 is an open calibration question.

methodologyNotes.name: Why Famine Risk Metrics and Famine Declarations Are Different
methodologyNotes.description: Somalia's Buur Hakaba district has an acute malnutrition rate of 37.1 percent — above the level that meets the famine malnutrition definition. But the IPC/FSNAU has not yet issued a formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration. The benchmark requires the formal declaration, not the precursor metric. The reason: a precursor metric is a forecast of likely conditions; a formal IPC Phase 5 declaration is a concluded determination by a recognized treaty-body-equivalent authority that famine exists. The benchmark treats the declaration as the adjudicated-equivalent event. This is not leniency — it is the methodology applying the same evidence standard it applies everywhere: concluded determinations move scores, not forecasts.

methodologyNotes.name: Eight False-Positive Screens in One Night — A New Record
methodologyNotes.description: Tonight's screening check stopped eight signals — double the prior record of four, set June 25. The eight covered all six of the major false-positive categories: (1) floor-limitation, (2) attribution check, (3) double-count, (4) stale-baseline compound, (5) pre-adjudication holds, and (6) threshold-check. No two used the same rule. The §3e-bis screening architecture is designed to catch different failure modes, not just one. Tonight it caught all six categories simultaneously — the most diverse single-cycle activation in the benchmark's history. Scores held at their correct levels because the architecture held.

forwardTriggers.trigger: End-of-June famine declaration window for Buur Hakaba district. Acute malnutrition rate (37.1 percent GAM) now above famine threshold. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is a scored change trigger for Somalia (4.7 of 100).
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of the June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.

July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window. First EU-imported case confirmed in France June 24. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
EU AI Act full applicability — general-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI as of this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability — same obligations as above apply. No public compliance roadmap from OpenAI as of this cycle.
August IPC lean-season update — IPC Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy. The lean season peak runs through August.

RSF commencement of direct assault on El Obeid residential zones — the El Fasher pattern of drone strikes then direct assault killing 6,000 civilians in three days is the documented comparator. HRW demanded UNSC action June 26.
Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review — Mali (12.5) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Flag in third day; no timeline set.
42-state attorney general proceedings and Florida criminal investigation — no fixed hearing dates; any concluded finding, settlement, or criminal charge constitutes a scored change trigger.

Next US Commerce or BIS action on the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order. The June 26 Congressional deadline elapsed with no public response from Commerce Secretary Lutnick. Suspension remains in effect.

Whether OCHA or IOM can establish alternative food-delivery mechanisms following WFP's forced termination of all 365 northern-area staff contracts. Houthi-controlled northern Yemen now lacks primary food-agency infrastructure.
Extended famine window — credible risk in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions through September 2026. Each new IPC Phase 5 designation in Somalia is a scored change trigger.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: The UN Commission of Inquiry (June 23) finds Israel 'continues to commit genocide' by deliberately targeting Palestinian children — approximately 20,000 killed. Israel holds the minimum score of 0 of 100. Does the genocide finding establish a new sub-anchor for the Equity dimension, or does the minimum score absorb all categories of adverse evidence?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Strongest legal-category finding in the benchmark record — issued for an entity already at the absolute floor.
